data.title: FPF: Preferred Stocks Also Have A U.S. Dollar Problem
data.description:
The First Trust Intermediate Duration Preferred & Income Fund (NYSE:FPF) is a closed-end fund, or CEF, that income-seeking investors might find attractive as a way of earning a very high level of current income. The fund certainly does pretty well in this respect, as it boasts a 9.10% yield that is far superior to the yield of most fixed-income indices. This can be clearly seen in this chart:

Index/ETF

Current Yield

Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index (AGG)

3.84%

Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index (JNK)

6.64%

ICE Exchange-Listed Preferred and Hybrid Securities Index (PFF)

6.70%

The most comparable of these indices to the First Trust Intermediate Duration Preferred and Income Fund is likely the ICE Exchange-Listed Preferred and Hybrid Securities Index. As we can see, that index only has a 6.70% current yield, which is quite a bit below the 9.10% yield of the closed-end fund. Income-focused investors will quite often prefer a higher yield to a lower one due to the simple fact that higher-yielding securities provide a higher level of income. In the case of this fund versus the index, a $1 million investment will generate about $91,000 annually from the First Trust Intermediate Duration Preferred and Income Fund compared to $67,000 annually from the ICE Exchange-Listed Preferred and Hybrid Securities Index. Obviously, the closed-end fund is the better option for anyone who is seeking to maximize their income.

The First Trust Intermediate Duration Preferred and Income Fund has also historically compared reasonably well with its peers in terms of yield. This table shows how it compares today:

Fund Name

Morningstar Classification

Current Yield

First Trust Intermediate Duration Preferred & Income Fund

Fixed Income – Taxable-Preferreds

9.10%

Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund (PSF)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Preferreds

7.68%

Flaherty & Crumrine Dynamic Preferred and Income Fund (DFP)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Preferreds

6.95%

John Hancock Preferred Income Fund (HPI)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Preferreds

9.23%

Nuveen Preferred & Income Opportunities Fund (JPC)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Preferreds

10.06%

The median fund out of this group in terms of yield is the First Trust Intermediate Duration Preferred & Income Fund. This particular fund has a yield that is substantially higher than that of two of its peers, and while it is also lower than two of them, we can see that it is not significantly lower than the yield of the John Hancock Preferred Income Fund. Indeed, a slight amount of movement on the part of either of these funds could reverse the relative yields and make the First Trust Intermediate Duration Preferred and Income Fund end up with the higher yield. There is no guarantee that this will be the case, of course, but it is always a possibility. Overall, though, we can see that this fund compares reasonably well with other preferred stock funds in terms of yield.

This is generally a good sign for income-oriented investors. After all, the simple fact that this fund’s yield is reasonably in line with its peers is a clear indication that the market does not anticipate that it will need to cut the payout in the near future. If it did have that belief, then the fund would actually have a far higher yield than its peers since the market would already be pricing in the distribution cut. This does not appear to be the case here.

Problems With Preferred Stocks In General

With that said, there is another form of distribution cut that the market does not appear to be considering. In a recent article, I presented the idea of a “stealth default” with respect to bonds. In this situation, the issuing entity does not default in the normal sense, as it does still make the mandatory coupon payments and repays the principal at maturity. However, it does so with a currency that is worth far less than what was originally lent. From that article:

This comes back to the idea that the currency that an investor receives when the bond matures is going to be worth a lot less than what was originally lent. It is a default in all but technicality. We have seen this over the past decade, as the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index delivered a negative total return to investors.

The First Trust Intermediate Duration Preferred and Income Fund might have this problem as well. While it invests more heavily in preferred stocks than in bonds, preferred stocks have the same problem as bonds in that they are exposed to a declining U.S. dollar. Most preferred stocks, after all, do not include any sort of protection against inflation. Rather, these securities simply pay out a specific amount of nominal U.S. dollars at specific intervals.

Thus, what happens is that as the U.S. dollar declines over time, the purchasing power of the dividends paid by preferred stocks also declines. This would also apply to the fund’s distribution, as merely sustaining its distribution means that investors will find that the distributions will buy fewer and fewer items in the real world over time. Thus, while the market does not seem to think that this fund will need to cut its distribution in a nominal sense, it is not pricing for the likelihood that the purchasing power of the distributions will decline over time.

We have already seen this scenario of a declining U.S. dollar playing out. The U.S. dollar index (DXY), which measures the value of the U.S. dollar against a basket of six foreign currencies (the euro, the Swiss franc, the Japanese yen, the Canadian dollar, the British pound, and the Swedish krona), has fallen by 8.44% year-to-date:

Anyone who has visited a country that uses one of these currencies over the past six months or so has certainly noticed this, as the price for everything abroad has risen when measured in U.S. dollar terms.

We have also seen the U.S. dollar fall precipitously in terms of gold (XAUUSD:CUR), platinum (XPTUSD:CUR), and silver (XAGUSD:CUR). This chart shows the year-to-date U.S. dollar-denominated prices of gold (black line), platinum (blue line), and silver (orange line):

As clearly shown, the U.S. dollar has fallen more than 20% year-to-date against all three precious metals. This table also summarizes the above chart:

Metal

YTD Price Change in US Dollars

Gold

28.39%

Silver

24.75%

Platinum

23.97%

We can also see how silver and platinum have apparently gone parabolic since mid-May, with both metals quickly accelerating. We can speculate all day why this is the case, but the steep appreciation of all three precious metals clearly indicates that the market is very worried about the long-term value of the U.S. dollar and its ability to act as a store of value. For reasons just discussed, this greatly reduces the attractiveness of any fixed-income security that is denominated in U.S. dollars. That would, unfortunately, include most of the securities that are included in the portfolio of the First Trust Immediate Duration Preferred and Income Fund.

There could be some reasons to expect that the devaluation of the U.S. dollar will continue. In early April, Fortune magazine ran an article stating that U.S. President Donald Trump wants the dollar to weaken against foreign currencies in order to improve the competitiveness of U.S. exporters. He is not incorrect in the assertion that a weaker dollar will likely improve the international competitiveness of American-made goods. Basically, if the dollar declines against foreign currencies, then it essentially makes American exports cheaper for foreigners who will be using their own native currencies. However, it does still mean that investors in fixed-income securities will be getting paid back in a weaker currency and thus the dividend payments paid by preferred stocks (and preferred stock funds such as the First Trust Intermediate Duration Preferred and Income Fund) will essentially decline over time in terms of the actual value of the dividends receives. Investors who are considering any sort of fixed-income security or fund in the current environment.

Finally, it is worth noting that if the Federal Reserve reduces interest rates in the near future, it may accelerate the decline in the U.S. dollar. As Forex Traders points out, a central bank cutting interest rates will usually reduce the value of a given currency relative to other assets. This is due to the carry trade, which is a common currency trading strategy that involves a trader borrowing currency with a low interest rate and using it to purchase currency with a higher interest rate. The trader makes money from the difference between the borrowing and lending rates. This concept is the reason why the Bank of Japan’s attempt to raise the interest rate on the yen caused havoc in all corners of the capital markets last August. As of right now, the market expects that the Federal Reserve will cut interest rates by 50 basis points by the end of 2025:

The market might already be pricing this into the relative valuation of the U.S. dollar versus other currencies, but if it does not (or if the Federal Reserve cuts by more than expected), then we could see the U.S. dollar weaken even more than it already has.

In other words, it seems almost certain that the value of the U.S. dollar will fall in the coming months and years. This weakens the case for holding a fixed-income fund like the First Trust Intermediate Duration Preferred and Income Fund.

Recent Performance Analysis

We previously discussed the First Trust Intermediate Duration Preferred and Income Fund on March 21, 2025. There have certainly been a few developments in the market since that time, a few of which were discussed in the preceding section.

The market has certainly not missed the general trend of the U.S. government and monetary authorities towards weakening the U.S. dollar. We can see this quite clearly by looking at the interest rates on long-term bonds. Consider the following yield movements:

Bond Term

Yield on December 31, 2024

Yield on March 21, 2025

Yield Today

Ten-Year

4.58%

4.25%

4.40%

Twenty-Year

4.86%

4.6%

4.90%

Thirty-Year

4.78%

4.59%

4.88%

This chart specifically shows the yields of U.S. Treasury securities with the specified term. Other types of bonds are not considered. We can see a trend at the long end towards rising interest rates, especially since March. This is consistent with the view that the market is beginning to get worried about the U.S. dollar’s ability to hold its value over the long term. Bond investors, in short, are worried that the currency that they receive back when the bond matures will be worth a lot less than it is worth today.

The only real way to protect against this is to demand a higher yield today that gives the investor more money today that can be used to purchase additional assets and thus have greater wealth at some future date. Personally, though, I still think that the current yields are far too low to actually accomplish this goal.

The price of fixed-rate securities rises whenever yields decline, and vice versa. This concept holds true for both bonds and preferred securities. As we can see from the chart above, yields have risen since our last discussion on the First Trust Intermediate Duration Preferred and Income Fund back in March. As such, we might expect the shares of this fund to have disappointed in terms of performance.

This is certainly the case, as we can see here:

As we can see, the shares of the First Trust Intermediate Duration Preferred and Income Fund have decreased by 2.53% since our last discussion on this fund. Interestingly, this is a better performance than the ICE Exchange-Listed Preferred and Hybrid Securities Index delivered over the period, which is quite surprising. After all, the fund uses leverage, so we would normally expect it to decline more than the comparable index does whenever asset prices decline. However, we do see that the fund declined much more than the preferred stock index did during the initial decline in early April, after which both the preferred stock index and the First Trust Intermediate Duration Preferred and Income Fund started to recover. The fund, in this case, recovered more strongly than the index did. This is not entirely out of line with what we would expect from a leveraged fund given this market action, although it is still surprising that it beat the index on a price-performance basis overall.

Now, it is worth noting that this fund outperformed the preferred stock index by a lot more when we look at it on a total return basis. This is clearly visible here:

As shown, investors in the First Trust Intermediate Duration Preferred and Income Fund only had a 0.27% total loss over the period that has passed since our last discussion on the fund. That is certainly nothing to get excited about, but it is much better than the 1.51% total loss that investors in the preferred stock suffered. As is frequently the case when a closed-end fund declines in price, the distributions that it paid out were sufficient to offset a significant proportion of the fund’s price decline. This is nice, but it is still worth pointing out that the U.S. dollar has been losing value against both precious metals and foreign currencies over the entire period shown above, so the fund’s performance on a real basis has been slightly worse than advertised. In short, investors sacrificed other opportunities over the period that did much better in U.S. dollar terms.

About The Fund

The website for the First Trust Intermediate Duration Preferred & Income Fund states that the fund has the primary objective of providing its investors with a very high level of current income. This makes sense given that the fund’s strategy revolves around purchasing and holding preferred stocks. From the website:

The Fund seeks to achieve its objectives by investing, under normal market conditions, at least 80% of its managed assets in preferred securities and other income producing securities.

Preferred stocks are much like bonds in that they deliver nearly all of their total returns to investors via direct payments that constitute income. We can see this very clearly by comparing the price performance to the total return performance of the iShares Preferred and Income Securities ETF, which tracks the ICE Exchange-Listed Preferred and Hybrid Securities Index, over the past ten years:

Certainly on a price-performance basis, preferred stocks did very poorly as an asset class over the past decade. Overall, the ICE Exchange-Listed Preferred and Hybrid Securities Index declined by 22.89% and obviously handed its investors a loss. However, we can see that, on a total return basis, investors in the index still got 36.03%. The distributions were in this case sufficient to completely offset the price decline of the index and still leave investors with a small profit. Granted, a 36.03% total return over ten years is not impressive at all (and it pales in comparison to most other things in the market), but at least it is a profit.

The most important thing here, though, is that all of the total returns that investors realized over the ten-year period came directly from the dividends and distributions that the index paid out over the period. Dividends and distributions are income to the investors, so we can clearly see that the current income objective of the First Trust Intermediate Duration Preferred and Income Fund makes a great deal of sense. This is how these securities deliver their returns, after all.

The First Trust Intermediate Duration Preferred and Income Fund does appear to be holding most of its assets in preferred stocks. The fund’s first-quarter 2025 holdings report provides the following asset allocation:

Asset Type

% of Net Assets

$25 Par Preferred Securities

22.8%

$1,000 Par Preferred Securities

3.8%

Capital Preferred Securities

122.8%

Exchange-Traded Funds

1.4%

Reverse Repurchase Agreement

(8.5%)

The fund is only holding one exchange-traded fund, the Invesco Preferred ETF (PGX):

As such, the fund’s exchange-traded fund position strengthens the preferred stock exposure of the portfolio beyond that already provided by the other preferred stocks held by the fund.

The Invesco Preferred ETF is certainly an interesting choice as an exchange-traded fund for the First Trust Intermediate Duration Preferred and Income Fund to gain exposure to an index of preferred stocks. Over the past ten years, the Invesco Preferred ETF has underperformed the similar iShares Preferred and Income Securities ETF:

As shown, over the past ten years, the Invesco Preferred ETF has declined by 24.45% compared to only a 22.89% decline for the iShares equivalent. On a total return basis, the difference is even more stark, as the iShares funds delivered a 36.03% total return compared to only 32.87% for the Invesco Preferred ETF. Finally, the iShares Preferred and Income Securities ETF has a 6.70% yield compared to the 6.18% yield of the Invesco Preferred ETF. As such, the Invesco Preferred ETF appears to be the worst of the two options, yet this is the one that this fund’s management opted to go with.

Earlier in this article, I stated that the U.S. dollar could be somewhat undesirable as a currency denomination for any fixed-income security of significant length. Preferred stocks tend to be perpetual in nature and do not have a maturity date, so they certainly qualify as having a significant length. With that said, the weighted average duration of the preferred stocks in the fund’s portfolio is only 4.96 years:

This is not really too bad, as it implies that investors will get their money back in just under five years. According to Investopedia:

Duration measures how long it takes, in years, for an investor to be repaid a bond’s price through its total cash flows.

The recent steepening of the U.S. Treasury yield curve seems to suggest that the market believes that the problems with the U.S. dollar will not emerge until the 2030s, so this 4.96-year average duration still provides time for investors to get their money back and get into better stores of value before the worst-case scenario occurs. Personally, I agree and suspect that the status quo will continue until the early 2030s, so this could be alright. With that said, we could very well see further deterioration of the U.S. dollar’s fundamentals well before that date.

The First Trust Intermediate Duration Preferred and Income Fund is primarily invested in U.S. dollar-denominated preferred stocks right now:

As shown, American issuers (who would all be using U.S. dollars as the currency to pay dividends to preferred stockholders) accounted for 59.14% at the end of April. Issuers in other countries accounted for the remainder of the fund’s portfolio (about 40.86%). It is uncertain whether these foreign issuers are using U.S. dollars or their own native currencies to pay the dividends on the preferred stocks that are held by this portfolio. The fund’s holding report lists the value of every security in U.S. dollars, but that does not necessarily mean that the fund is actually receiving U.S. dollars in dividend payments on all of them (it might be converting their foreign currency values into U.S. dollars for reporting purposes). If indeed the foreign issuers are paying their dividends in foreign currency, then it could be a net benefit to the fund.

In short, the exact opposite effect would be happening here to what happens when U.S. citizens travel abroad. Whenever the fund receives euros (or whatever currency the preferred stock happens to be paying its dividends in) and then goes to convert it back into U.S. dollars, the same amount of currency will convert into more U.S. dollars than it did in the past. Basically, for American investors in a declining U.S. dollar environment, the dividends on foreign-currency-denominated preferred stocks would increase over time even though they stay the same when denominated in foreign currency.

Depending on the actual exposure that this fund has to foreign currency-denominated preferred stocks, this could be a real advantage for the fund going forward, and it does improve its attractiveness somewhat compared to a fund that has most or all of its assets invested in U.S. dollar-denominated securities. The fund does not actually disclose how much of its assets are in U.S. dollar-denominated securities as opposed to foreign ones, though. All that we know for sure is that 59.14% of its portfolio consists of securities that were issued by American entities. Thus, the fund’s foreign currency exposure could be anywhere from 0% to 40.86%.

Valuation

Shares of the First Trust Intermediate Duration Preferred and Income Fund are currently trading at a 3.49% discount on net asset value. This is reasonably in line with the 3.28% discount on net asset value that the shares have had on average over the past month.

The fund’s shares are currently trading at a price that is somewhat worse than their long-term historical average price. Over the past three years, shares of the First Trust Intermediate Duration Preferred and Income Fund have traded at prices ranging from a 0.78% discount to a 16.56% discount on net asset value. The average price over the three-year period has been a 7.96% discount on net asset value, which is a much better price than the fund has right now. This suggests that investors might be able to get in at a better price if they are patient and wait a little bit.

Here is how the current price of the First Trust Intermediate Duration Preferred and Income Fund compares with its peers:

Fund Name

Premium/(Discount)

First Trust Intermediate Duration Preferred & Income Fund

(3.49%)

Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund

(7.54%)

Flaherty & Crumrine Dynamic Preferred and Income Fund

(7.72%)

John Hancock Preferred Income Fund

2.48%

Nuveen Preferred & Income Opportunities Fund

0.13%

As was the case with these five funds in terms of yield, the First Trust Intermediate Duration Preferred & Income Fund is the median one in terms of price. There are two funds shown here that trade at premiums, which is very hard to justify given the current problems facing any fixed-income security. There are also two funds that are trading at larger discounts (and at prices that are much closer to the long-term average price of the First Trust Intermediate Duration Preferred and Income Fund.

Overall, the price of the fund does not look terrible right now, especially when compared to its peers. However, I would still prefer to get it at a much larger discount on net asset value than it has right now since that will take some of the sting out of the U.S. dollar devaluation that will cause the distributions to weaken over time.

Expenses

The First Trust Intermediate Duration Preferred and Income Fund has an expense ratio of 4.47% on net assets. The majority of the expenses are due to the interest payments that the fund has to make on the money that it has borrowed in order to obtain leverage. If these costs are excluded, the fund’s expense ratio drops to 1.39%:

These are the same figures that this fund had the last time that we discussed it, so there are no updates to make in this respect.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the First Trust Intermediate Duration Preferred and Income Fund suffers from some of the same problems currently plaguing all U.S. dollar-denominated fixed-income securities. This is that the currency in which these securities are denominated is declining, which means that investors ultimately get back less than they originally lent. This fund’s duration helps a bit with this as the securities that it holds should pay back the initial investment in just under five years, which should be fast enough to avoid the serious problems likely coming in the 2030s. The fact that this fund has more than 40% of its assets invested in foreign-issue securities should also help, assuming that the foreign securities are denominated in a foreign currency.

Overall, this fund might be okay if it had a larger discount, but at the current price, it does not seem to have enough of a margin of safety priced in to cover the U.S. dollar depreciation problem.

data.title: What DoD's Close Relationship With Palantir Means For Investors
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The Pentagon raised the ceiling of the Maven Smart System contract, awarded to Palantir, by $795 million, bringing the aggregate value of the contract close to $1.3 billion. The rise manifests Department of Defense's (DoD) surging demand for Palantir's analytics software for the remainder of the contract, which runs through 2029. This begs the question: how the upcoming DoD contracts can shape Palantir’s growth and its stock price. The stock currently offers investors a moderate upside potential, with a fair value of $162.65.

Palantir delivered an incredible performance in Q1 2025. The US revenue was up 55% YoY to reach a total of $628 million. Adjusted EPS hit the $0.13 mark, signalling a 62.5% increase YoY. And free cash flow margin shot to 42% in Q1 2025 from 23% in Q1 2024.

Company Overview

Palantir is essentially a big data analytics software company whose software products help government agencies and private enterprises deduce a recognizable pattern from millions of raw unstructured data set. Palantir's Gotham arm serves government agencies and military, and the Foundry arm caters the decision-making needs of private enterprises.

Contract Opportunities

JADC2

Joint All-Domain Command and Control (JADC2) is an initiative by the DoD that aims to deploy a central connectivity among all of its branches in terms of the databases and communications. They will likely implement JADC2 across the next several years at an estimate of implementation costs running to billions of dollars. Palantir seems to be the flyer on JADC2, is, in fact, understanding the structure of the opportunity for development. Therefore, it goes without saying that JADC2 implementation will be done with Palantir's support.

JADC2 implementation seems to be more or less like listing the principal challenges of the overall integration platforms of JADC2. Initial phases aim for specifying interconnectivity between already bewildered systems, to first show that there is a possibility for data flow of subjected formats between the aforementioned arrangements. The later stages will, in fact, add analytical functionalities that help commanders make sense of the integrated information streams. The last phase, in fact, is the implementation of advanced decision support tools capable of processing with multi-domain information and giving recommendations in real time.

Palantir's experience allows them to be the best firm for JADC2 contracts. Their platform is already very popular and is tested to integrate a plethora of military systems like unseen and seen intelligence databases, logistics, and informational networks among many others. What is more technical are the JADC2 problems now such as - through multi-source streaming of data, maintaining security across various classification levels, and visualizing of data meaning complex interfaces - but they are all things that Palantir can easily deal with.

Scope JADC2 gives room not only for prime contractor roles but also for the specialization of subcontractor jobs. Possible roles of prime contractors may include an overall system architecture, data integration frameworks, or user interface development. Subcontractor roles could focus on the integration of specific sensors or algorithms to create specialized analytical conditions.

The geographical distribution of JADC2 will be implemented and create a variety of contract opportunities. The system must run within combat commands worldwide, which are under different operational requirements and have different system configurations. Thus, regional implementation contracts arise as they adapt the JADC2 protocol architecture to the respective operational environments.

ABMS Evolution

The Advanced Battle Management System is the U.S. Air Force's move to aligning with JADC2. It is worth specifying the main features of ABMS aiming at air and space domain operations. The unearthed ABMS characteristics push that area as deployed, leveraging Palantir's efficiency in the real-time data processing and decision-making mechanics.

Thus, integrating different types of sensors on military aircraft, making correlated move and situational awareness displays under very high-stress conditions are what Air domain awareness entails. In particular, the information related to fast-moving aircraft is particularly problematic due to handling the high-velocity data streams of the aircraft and sensor products typically having different update rates and accuracy. Moreover, the aircrews are being presented with situation awareness information, which is of critical importance at the time of so much stress.

Air operations juggling normally with time targets need meanwhile operations with sub-seconds critical processing. Aircraft speeding at supersonic levels are miles away –prove real-time processing a must for balanced operation. Palantir's capabilities emphasize the streaming architecture and real-time processing to attend to these time constraints.

The spatial nature of air activity also introduces some technical issues. Air operations, which push limitations of deciding from the ground, must provide tools to each aircraft that collaborate in the command with less bandwidth and less reliable communication. This presupposes analytical abilities that work in disconnected environments as well as keep synchronizing on the communication paths.

ABMS constructions will involve many contracts with different utilities, such as air domain integration. Sensor integration and basic data sharing are the priorities, whereas later phases predictive analytics, automated threat assessment, and advanced decision support features are not missed out. Each phase then offers an opportunity for companies which have the corresponding know-how.

Intelligence Community Integration Projects

The modernization of the Intelligence Community will, in fact, be paving the path for Palantir based on its established experience and strong capabilities. These projects will mainly focus on enhancing information sharing, as well as collaboration in analytical work and decision support across various intelligence agencies.

Old intelligence affected operations by companion agencies not being connected and focused on different data collecting methods or at different time frames. The modern threats need the integrated analysis including human intelligence, signals intelligence, geospatial intelligence, and open-source intelligence sources in a single report. This integration challenge with the multi-domain operations for military commands is the same, but the former comes with the extra difficulties for security reasons and the inter-agency coordination.

Palantir's Gotham platform has already shown the efficiency of intelligence integration, thus creating a base for further capabilities. The household requirements will be met with the collaboration tools promoting real-time connectivity that lets the analysts of the two agencies to unite to work on pressing intelligence problems while respecting the security boundary.

An area that really opens the door for advancement is Artificial Intelligence in intelligence analysis, especially in both-human and machine relationships. The intelligence report should be easy to read through and provide clear logic of reasoning together with the references that support the reasoning. Therefore, requiring the explainability aspects even more than in other military applications. Hence, Palantir's manner of developing AI technology is in line with the Array requirements and entails democratizing human beings and machines and making the right decisions together.

The international scope of intelligence operations requires specialized systems that will work in both very secure locations in the United States and temporary ones that have little infrastructure. Thus, Palantir's containerized deployment model allows for consistent analytical capabilities across diverse operational environments.

Valuation - DCF Model

The WACC of 8.31% in my calculations is in line with the WACC of 9.69% for Aswath Damodaran's Software (System & Application) industry. This illustrates in, in general, the moderate cost of capital that software companies incur due to the high growth potential the market perceives.

Palantir offers investors a moderate 25.1% growth opportunity. The model determines the fair value of $162.65 using reasonably conservative projections that are consistent with the company’s past median financial performances. In light of the paradigm shift the DoD is currently undergoing, this clearly illustrates Palantir’s earnings potential and its ability to use leverage to gain market share.

The Competitive Scene

The JEDI contract competition, which is the Joint Enterprise Defense Infrastructure contract competition, was won by Microsoft in 2019, although it was very much contended by AWS and hence, it proved the companies' serious commitment to defense markets well. However, that contract was terminated by DoD in 2021 due to unsatisfactory designs. AWS's technical capabilities in cloud computing, data storage, and distributed processing lay the groundwork for military data analytics applications. Their global infrastructure allows military operations to be executed in many places all over the world while keeping performance and security characteristics alike.

The Palantir's architecture philosophy is all about the development of integrated platforms that deliver the complete analytical solutions dealing with certain problems. Their platform is made of data integration, processing, analysis, visualization, and collaboration capabilities, which are designed to work together in a snap for military analytical tasks. This integrated approach grants military users the ability to run complex analytical operations based solely on one tool instead of multiple ones.

Microsoft and AWS are the companies that use the component-based architecture model, meaning that customers are the ones to combine the services. These platforms are best known for the delivery of quality individual services like, data storage, processing, machine learning, visualization, collaboration, etc. However, it is a prerequisite for the users to integrate them to have a complete analytical toolkit. Disparate component integration is tailored to customers who have a varied range of needs; however, it requires exceptional dedication for the integration process.

Military customer preference for integrated solutions over component-based approaches is rooted in the resource constraints and operational requirements. Most military forces usually lack the staff who are technically trained to run complex integrations between different services, while their operations can mostly work with solutions that require no maintenance or technical support during the deployment. Component-based solutions will always need continuous care and improvement, which may not be possible to do in the environments they have deployed.

Key Risks

Palantir faces the biggest risk due to its increasing reliance on government contracts, particularly those with the Department of Defense (DoD). A company is prone to lose all its customers when it relies on a large part of its income from one customer or customer segment that isn't purchasing or isn't making budget allocations that it used to do. The federal government's budget cycles are famous for their unpredictability. They are influenced by political pressure, the presence of continuing resolutions, and possible sequestration cuts, which can dramatically slash the amount of money allocated to defense.

By diversifying its contracts beyond the DoD, Palantir has also signed contracts with several other federal agencies such as the Department of Health and Human Services, the Internal Revenue Service, and other intelligence agencies. This entrenchment within the government sector implies that budget cuts or priority shifts in one agency do not necessarily have an effect on the entire revenue stream from the government. Imagine it as having multiple income sources from different jobs instead of relying on only one employer.

The organization has also focused on the growth of its commercial market and has included industries like healthcare, financial services, and manufacturing as targets. These commercial contracts follow different budgeting periods than government contracts and have their own decision-making processes, and thus they provide an inherent hedge against government expenditure volatility. Furthermore, for Q1 of FY 2025, Palantir’s US government revenue grew by 45% YoY and 9% QoQ to $373 million, US commercial revenue grew by 71% YoY and 19% QoQ to $255 million, signalling Palantir’s growing strides to expand its commercial business.

Recommendation

The particular contracts considered in this analysis have appeared as various elements of the same basic transformation: the military's shift from platform-centric to network-centric operations, larrgely dependent on information superiority and data-driven decision-making. The platform approach of Palantir makes them well-capable of dealing with many sides of this transition while utilizing one and the same technical infrastructure and expertise. I believe the 25.1% upside potential offered by Palantir’s stock puts investors in a formidable position to reap meaning gains for their respective portfolios.

data.title: Water Assets And Land Development: A Long-Term Infrastructure Play
data.description:
Pure Cycle Corporation (NASDAQ:PCYO) is an interesting case, a utility company that transformed into a land developer. In this analysis, I will explore the company’s evolution, its growth potential at Sky Ranch, and what this means for valuation and investors going forward.

The company

Pure Cycle Corporation started as a water utility, but later expanded into land and property development as opportunities came up throughout its history.

The company provided water and wastewater services for the land developers in Denver, Colorado, but in 2010 those land developers were unable to recover from the 2008 subprime mortgage crisis and had to sell the land to Pure Cycle at discounted prices. The acquired land, named Sky Ranch, is located east, almost outside of Denver, and is currently zoned to include up to 3,200 single family and multifamily homes.

The company is building a master planned community there and has already sold around 1,000 lots for home builders. Sky Ranch also has space for building recreational centers, schools, commercial and light industrial space, all of which will be connected to the company’s water and wastewater services, according to the company’s 2024 10-K. Once the master planned community is fully built and occupied, which is projected to be around 2030. Pure Cycle will still have enough water capacity to support more than ten similar planned communities in the area.

The company’s water assets are valuable in the Denver, since water is a scarce resource, but Denver’s population is increasing. The city also has some restrictions on outdoor water usage during the summer months. All of that contributes to the appreciation of Pure Cycle’s water assets in the long term.

The company provides water and wastewater for the local government entities, industrial, Oil and gas operations and customers at the Sky Ranch. Their wastewater segment is capable of recycling 100% of the returned water, excluding irrigation and other outdoor usage.

While Oil and Gas operators have been an important source of revenue for the company, representing 50% of the water revenues in 2024, it is also very unstable, depending on oil prices to operate.

The land Development segment is now rapidly increasing with their master planned community, Sky Ranch, but the land was acquired in 2010, and the company only began development in 2017. This is something that has frustrated older shareholders, the time it takes for the company to start delivering results.

In the land development segment, the company prepares the lots until it is ready for construction, and builds the public infrastructure around it, which they get reimbursed by the state afterwards. When the lots are sold to home builders, and they start a new construction, the company receives the tap fee, which is a connection fee. They do not build their rental houses themselves, but instead rely on the homebuilders already working on the land. Despite this, they still achieve good margins, spending around $350,000 to build a home valued at approximately $500,000. Finally, building rental houses could be an important source of recurring revenue for the company, they currently have 14 homes built, 17 are under construction and the company is planning 200 houses for when Sky Ranch is fully built.

Latest Results

Pure Cycle’s Q2 2025 net Income rose from $118,000 to $809,000, largely supported by $1,910,000 in revenue from oil and gas royalties. In contrast, the revenue from commercial customers, which comes mostly from the water usage of oil and gas companies, has plummeted almost 90% from the previous quarter. This is due to crude oil prices, which have declined to a point where it is no longer viable for companies to operate on Pure Cycle’s leased land. The company only receives royalty revenue after water is used for fracking and the oil and gas are sold. So, if there’s no water usage and oil prices stay at the current levels, there won’t be any royalty income in the future.

On the bright side, land development investments are paying off, with increased lot sales and increased water and wastewater tap fees. That is good for the company to accelerate recurring revenue from families on Sky Ranch and reduce dependency on oil and gas. On the flip side, general and administrative expenses increased at a concerning pace, 31% YoY and 35% QoQ. That is something to keep an eye on.

To summarize in a phrase, the increased revenue from lot sales and tap fees were barely enough to cover a cooling oil and gas market and increased general and administrative expenses.

Investment thesis

Pure Cycle has several positive aspects. They have massive potential due to their water capacity and their investments in land and houses that are inflation-protected assets and complement each other, adding to the company’s competitiveness.

The Sky Ranch community is expanding with attractive housing prices. The company wants to accelerate home deliveries and increase the number of residents in order to attract commercials to Sky Ranch, as Pure Cycle believes this will increase margins after commercial interests increase. Also, when the company builds public infrastructure, they are eligible to receive reimbursement for the costs incurred, but this reimbursement is only received after enough taxes are generated by residents and businesses, another incentive to populate the area.

One negative aspect of the thesis is that Pure Cycle does not pay dividends, which is usually expected by investors in the utility sector. The company is in a capital-heavy expansion process, also the CEO Mark Harding said during their latest webcast that the company could end up selling the rental houses if they see an opportunity to buy more land, which would further delay the company’s earnings and investors would have to wait to see the company buying back the stocks or paying dividends.

Risks to Consider

Pure Cycle’s growth really comes down to how well they execute. The slow pace of land development and their dependence on Denver’s housing market in the long run add some timing risks.

Oil and gas revenue can be pretty volatile, and if energy prices stay low for a while, that could hit their cash flow hard. Plus, even though their water rights are valuable in the long run, turning that value into actual cash will take time and patience.

Valuation

To project future revenues, I am assuming the same rate of construction and assuming that after finishing the current Sky Ranch Lots, the company will start developing the adjacent lots.

Revenues

Metered water usage: Divided into Municipal and Commercial customers, is a function of the water used and the number of single-family equivalent (SFE) water connections.

But 77% of the Commercial customers revenue come from Oil and Gas operators, and oil prices are currently falling to the point where in the latest quarter, Q2 2025, commercial customers receipts were $161,000 against $1,481,000 in the previous year, Q2 2024. Meaning that Oil and Gas companies are not operating at this current oil and gas prices.

So, to be conservative and to remove non-recurring effects of the projection, I am not considering receipts from Oil and Gas, but I will include them later in a best-case scenario. Therefore, I projected a 200 SFE increase per year, in line with the average from 2021 to 2024, which went from 855 to 1400 SFE water connections and I added a 2% YoY price increase to account for inflation.

Wastewater treatment fees: I have projected 130 SFE increase per year, in line with the average from 2021 to 2024, which went from 580 to 952 SFE wastewater connections and I added a 2% YoY price increase to account for inflation.

Water and wastewater tap fees: The company guided $5.8 million for 2025 and $19.8 million over the next three years.

I believe they will generate more than what was guided, due to the current trend of SFE connections whith the average price of $33,200 per SFE and $8,000 per wastewater tap, disclosed in 2024’ 10K. But to be conservative I have projected the next 3 years according to their guide, and the following years are projected according to the last 4 years average of $4.1 million and increasing with a 2% inflation rate.

Lot sales: The company guided 228 lots scheduled for delivery in fiscal 2025, and an additional 364 lots scheduled for delivery in fiscal 2026 at Sky Ranch. In the Q4 2024 Earnings Call, it was also said that around 700 lots will be delivered in a three-year period.

Also, Lot prices have been increasing “a lot”. In 2022, 219 lots were sold at an average rate of $55,400 per lot and in 2024 the average was $75,800 per lot, which is a 16.7% YoY increase. So, to project lot sales I am considering a rate of 200 lots per year after 2027, and a 7% price increase YoY.

Project management fees: A fraction of the lot sales, this account has averaged 4.5% of the lot sales, and that was what I considered for the projection.

Single-family rentals: The company shared guidance of building 100 rental homes in the next three to five years and has signaled that they want to continue building rental homes in the coming years, aiming at 200 to 300 homes.

In this account, I have considered 2025 to add the already in-construction 17 homes, then I have added 35 homes in 2026 and 2027 to achieve the 100 houses mark that was guided, afterwards I have considered 20 homes every year. And I have added a price increase of 3.5%, which is the 20 years compounded average rental price growth rate for Denver.

Special facility projects and other: In this account I have used the last 4 years’ growth rate, which is 13.1%, as a base for comparison, and projected at a 10% growth rate.

Those assumptions resulted in an average revenue growth rate of 9.3%, and I believe the company is capable of achieving better results, even without receipts from Oil and Gas.

Projecting the other accounts

Cost of goods sold decreasing by 0.2% as a percentage of revenues, in line with the current trend of reducing costs. SG&A as a percentage of revenue decreasing by 0.1% YoY, continuing the current trend of cost reduction.

Depreciation and amortization in line with the last years. Interest expense equal to last year's. Income tax rate equal to last year's.

Day sales outstanding [DSO], equal to last year’s. Days inventory outstanding [DIO], equal to last year’s. Days payable outstanding [DPO], equal to last year’s.

Other Items, estimated by the current other items costs' trend increase and diluting the $61 million estimated by the company to be received as reimbursement for public infrastructure built. A CapEx according to the last 4-year average.

I have not considered the issuance or repayments of new debt, or equity issuance or buybacks.

With the valuation date being May 30, 2025, and the perpetuity growth rate of 3.5%, based on the previously used 20 year rental inflation rate for Denver.

Assuming that the 2024 EV/EBITDA of 13.05 will be constant, the fair value per stock for Pure Cycle Corporation is $10.60. Resulting in a 0% upside, given the current price of $10.62. For a more conservative scenario, the fair value per stock would be $9.81, a downside of 7.6%. And in a bullish case, also Including Oil and Gas receipts, the fair value per stock would be $12.00, a 13.0% upside.

Conclusion

Pure Cycle offers stability with a compelling mix of utility and real estate development. However, having the water assets but not enough customers yet means that the company will require considerable time and continued investment to fully monetize. Additionally, in the future, the company may consider selling the rental properties in order to finance land acquisitions to support long-term growth, which would reduce free cash flow in the short term. Overall, Pure Cycle is a long-term investment and, based on cash flows, appears fairly valued. The stock is a Hold.

data.title: Olo: Mixed Signals Call For Hold
data.description:
Super returns in the past year of ~85% and a subscription based SaaS play drives attention to Olo Inc (NYSE:OLO), particularly after the blockbuster start post pandemic. Management's toned down expectation in 2023 citing implementation challenges explained a lot of the correction after the initial euphoria. But is Olo set now for the growth it promised? The business model around high margins SaaS offerings looks good on paper, but there are resistances to the thesis from competition. Let us dive deep to see whether the investment case is supported, particularly now after the run up in the past year.

Margins, Topline and Subscriptions

As of Q1 2025, Olo reported almost all of its revenue from its subscription based platform segment (1-2% from professional services). The subscription model also saw a 12% YoY increase in ARPU.

The first pillar of a Buy thesis suffers when I look at gross margins. A consistently slowing gross margin well below expected levels for a subscription revenue driven SaaS company is concerning. Most likely an offshoot of aggressive pricing and incentivizing onboarding new clients. However, the customer count does not reflect aggressive pricing resulting in market share capture either.

On double clicking the revenue growth, most of it has been supported by a growing ARPU. This coupled with shrinking gross margins point to new offerings that are dependent on third party costs (such as those dependent on delivery or payment). Also if the uptick is due to usage based revenue, that too comes with variables costs such as Stripe fees, for example. All in all, the revenue mix in the growing ARPU is not higher margin.

Contrary to what would have been a better investment thesis, where a good gross margin and ailing EBITDA margin would have made sense, EBITDA margins are showing a rise into positive territory over the past year or so. With no visible customer growth commensurate to such scale benefits, the operating leverage could be stemming from cost discipline and the fact that upselling (resulting in ARPU driven growth) leverages lesser operational costs than acquiring new customers and functionalities. At a time, when R&D expenses and even Sales and Marketing could be ramped up to convince a growth mindset, EBITDA positive margins are good news but the wrong margin to attack first. I believe EBITDA margin improvements without product investment or customer success may hurt growth and gross margin long term.

Profitability and Cash Flow vs Growth Ceiling

The free cash flow has also trickled into positive territory recently. Focus on profitability has helped a strong FCF yield on revenue (mid twenties). I have already emphasized on the lack of growth drivers driving the profitability uptrend. However, there are some green shoots here. The product market fit is established when existing customers are receptive to upselling efforts. The stickiness and value in Olo's offerings are strong given the magnitude of ARPU growth. Profitability first before customer acquisition is a great way to approach more fuel in the tank in the form of cash flow to fund further customer acquisition and even R&D. However, if the strategy is forced due to demand issues or market penetration challenges, it could point to competitive risks. The healthy FCF is good, but without scale, Olo will continue to run as a small business, profitably but limited. There are concentration risks, growth ceilings to an ARPU led approach or even addressable market saturation concerns till Olo reaches out in a broader market.

Insights from the Quarterly Report Q1 2025

Net Revenue Retention is decent at 111% as reported in Q1 2025. This is not best in class for SaaS companies, but decent for a constant churn food segment. Guidance on revenue for 2025 stands at $339m at the midpoint, not a huge growth era coming, but expectedly feeling the heat from an uncertain macro particularly on IT spends.

On new focus areas and products, Olo is expanding its payment solutions to include card-present transactions, enhancing its offerings beyond online payments. Olo aims to achieve $110m in revenue from its Olo Pay platform in 2025. New partnerships, including with Chipotle, are good indicators but the top line guidance does not reflect significant growth, potentially expecting churn in my opinion. I expect churn in 2025 both due to temporary factors related to the uncertain macro, and also structural as the food industry is prone to closures and cost optimizations as they mature. Olo should expect to face competition not only from Toast, Square and DoorDash, but also from larger players building capabilities in house for better control and cost dynamics. Food remains a tough industry for SaaS unless bigger players are onboard.

Valuations

Olo’s valuation at 3.1x EV/Forward Revenue is notably lower than PAR’s at 6.4x and Toast at 3.8x due to several factors. PAR’s higher multiple reflects its position as a full-stack POS provider with strong customer stickiness, leading to higher growth expectations and platform control. Toast's valuation is curiously low, probably because of its sustained profitability concerns.

Olo is seen primarily as middleware connecting ordering and delivery, with less control over the core restaurant technology stack, which limits its valuation. While Olo is expanding into payments (Olo Pay) and card-present transactions, this strategy is still evolving and therefore not priced in.

Olo's valuation looks reasonable, although it is at the high end of its own historical averages. The recent run-up on the back of profitability needs more top line validation before rerating or multiple expansion looks possible.

Almost a Sell

I am concerned about Olo's declining gross margins and lack of meaningful customer growth despite solid ARPU increases. The shrinking margins suggest rising third-party costs and aggressive pricing, which challenges the narrative of a high-margin SaaS business. Additionally, the cautious revenue guidance for 2025 point to limited growth in a competitive and structurally challenging food PoS and tech market.

That said, I am not selling because Olo’s shift into payments and its strong subscription revenue base offer promising long term support. The company’s improving profitability, positive free cash flow and stickiness in upselling indicate some product-market fit and operational discipline.

Overall, I recommend a Hold. Investors should watch closely for clearer signs of top-line acceleration, further customer acquisition and sustained margin improvements before adding more exposure. A more compelling entry opportunity may arise if Olo can demonstrate stronger growth execution (particularly in payments) or if valuation contracts due to near term challenges.

data.title: Over 7.7% From Vornado Realty Trust And Its Preferred Stocks
data.description:
For quite a while, we avoided investing in fixed-rate preferred stocks because their spreads were too narrow. However, the spreads have recently become more reasonable, which has made us reconsider investing in REIT preferred stocks. We find them much more attractive now, primarily since we've focused on the safest options first, with the extra requirement of yields close to or above 6.5%. Today's idea is Vornado Realty Trust (NYSE:VNO) and its preferred stocks with over 7% current yield.

Company overview

Vornado Realty Trust is one of the largest REITs in the U.S., founded in 1982, and headquartered in New York City. VNO has total assets are around $16 billion and a market capitalization of $8.17 billion. The capital structure is composed of 64% equity, 32% consolidated debt, and 4% preferred stocks:

VNO earns around 10% on rent. The asset coverage ratio is 168%. Excluding depreciation and amortization, operating expenses are around 56% of the total revenue. We calculate asset yield by dividing the total revenue by total assets and get a 12.4% asset yield. We also calculate market-adjusted asset yield to be more accurate. Therefore, we will need a price-to-book value, which is currently 1.85. We will subtract total equity from total assets and add the new one, multiplied by P/B. From here, we can calculate the market-adjusted yield by repeating the same operation: we get 9.24%. The market-adjusted asset coverage ratio is 224%. There are three types of instruments that investors can buy to gain exposure to a company's capital structure:

Common stock

The current price of the common stock is $39.15. You can see below the basic valuation metrics of the stock:

FFO per share is 2.16, and the forward P/FFO is 17.66. The forward FFO yield is 5.7%, so the higher this ratio, the better the credibility of debt and preferred stock. A high P/FFO ratio means that the public views the underlying business so highly that they are willing to pay a very high price to take advantage of the future growth in those earnings.

Preferred stocks

VNO has four preferred stocks:

(VNO.PR.O) was issued in 2021, and its call date is 09/22/2026. At the moment, it is trading at $14.37 with a current stripped yield of 7.84%:

(NYSE:VNO.PR.L) was issued in 2013, and its call date was in 2018, so it can be called at any time. At the moment, it is trading at $17.75 with a current stripped yield of 7.68%:

(NYSE:VNO.PR.M) was issued in 2017, and its call date was in 2022, and it is callable. At the moment, it is trading at $17.03 with a current stripped yield of 7.77%:

(NYSE:VNO.PR.N) was issued in 2020, and its call date is 11/24/2025. At the moment, it is trading at $15.95 with a current stripped yield of 7.84%:

Moody's gives them Ba3, S&P B+, and Fitch BB-, which are not the best credit scores, but are still decent. Let's compare it with its peers:

VNO's preferred shares outperform companies like LAND, SLG, BFS, and UMG.

Bonds

VNO has two outstanding publicly traded bonds, with yields of 6% and 5.4%. Moodys rates them with a Ba1 score and S&P with a BBB-.

Credibility

We will use Moody's methodology with a few adjustments:

1. Gross assets. VNO has close to $15 billion in gross assets, which puts it into the A1 category.

2. Unencumbered assets represent around 94% of all assets, which gives again a score of A1 for this metric.

3. Secured debt for the company stands in the Ba3 score.

4. Total debt + preferred stock as a percentage of gross assets gives us 47.11%, and a credit rating score equivalent to Baa3.

5. Net debt to EBITDA. We use the total debt here, and with an EBITDA of $0.8 billion, this is a worse Ca3 score.

6. Fixed charge coverage. EBITDA /(interest and dividend payment on its preferred stock). VNO gets a score of Ba1 here.

Based on these six "balance sheet" scores, VNO gets an overall score of 11.18, equivalent to a Ba2 score. The adjusted one is 10.67, which is a Ba1.

For "Market positioning and asset quality" and "Operating environment," we give a double A2 score.

Liquidity and access to capital

The operating expenses of the business, excluding amortization, are close to 57%, and we give VNO a B2 rating.

After calculating a score for market positioning, operating environment, and liquidity and adjusting all ratios based on the market-adjusted characteristics, we get a credit score of 10.15, which is equivalent to Baa3.

Conclusion

Vornado Realty Trust is one of the largest and most established REITs with a solid capital structure and a well-diversified portfolio. While the credit ratings of its preferred stock and bonds are not among the highest, they remain acceptable and demonstrate stability. The preferred stocks' yields, which exceed 7.5%, make them particularly attractive in the current market environment, where yields on similar instruments are often lower. VNO's financials point to good efficiency and solid debt coverage, although some metrics, such as net debt to EBITDA, suggest higher risk. However, the company has a strong market position and access to capital, which supports its better credit rating within the Baa3 framework.

data.title: Red Violet: Its Profitable Business Model Drives Revenue Growth
data.description:
Introduction

Red Violet Inc. (NASDAQ:RDVT) operates in the data and data analytics industry. The company doesn't report a separate segment. However, for my analysis, I based it on two key revenue streams that the company reports: Pricing Contracts and Transactional Customers. All revenues are derived from the U.S. market. Pricing contracts represent 77% of the company's total revenue, while Transactional Customers refer to the remaining 23%.

  • Pricing contracts are generally annual or longer-term contracts with auto-renewal and provide a more predictable revenue stream.
  • Transactional Customers refer to revenues coming from customers who pay to access the company's database without any contractual commitment; as a result, this is a more variable revenue stream.

RDVT has a market cap of $677 million and is currently trading at approximately $48 per share. I rate the stock as BUY, based on the following reasons: Strong financial, profitable business model and positive momentum.

Market Prospects

Overview of Industry

The data analytics industry is highly competitive. RDVT applies data analytics for specific purposes such as identity verification, risk mitigation, due diligence, fraud detection and prevention, and this industry has many well-established peers – like TransUnion (NYSE:TRU) and Thomson Reuters (NYSE:TRI) – as well as new players striving to gain market share. For the purpose of industry analysis, the landscape can be divided into two categories: 1) Data Analytics as a broad market and 2) the Fraud Detection and Prevention market, which includes the above company's core services.

Data Analytics

The global market size is valued at $64.99 billion in 2024 and is projected to grow to $402.7 billion by 2032, exhibiting a CAGR of 25.5% during this period, as this analysis illustrates. Also, North America accounted for the largest market share in 2022.

RDVT operates in an industry with significant room to grow and as the above analysis indicates, we are still at an early stage. Therefore, the company could benefit from this industry's growth and boost its revenue.

Fraud Detection and Prevention Market

With a global market size of $52.82 billion in 2024 and projections to grow to $246.16 billion by 2032, exhibiting a CAGR of 21.2% over the same period, as this analysis illustrates. Also, North America generated the largest revenue share in 2024.

RDVT has already "won" a share of the above markets, and its business model has demonstrated the ability to attract new customers. Investors should consider whether Red Violet has the potential for further expansion. Given the above prospects of these markets and the company's proven track record, l believe RDVT's long-term outlook appears more than promising.

Financial Analysis and Hidden Assets

Financial Analysis

RDVT already has a favorable track record regarding its revenue and debt performance. As the table below indicates, the company shows a clear downward trend in its already low-level debt, having reduced it by 32% since FY 2019. On the other hand, revenues have shown an impressive trend over the past few years, having grown by 148% over the same period. Even better, for FY 2024, all its debt comes from lease liabilities, with no Bank debt reported. This debt structure is very important for growth companies trying to capture the market, and the reason is that during periods of stress or uncertainty, the company is more flexible in overcoming such a situation.

As mentioned above, Red Violet operates in an industry with many large players, like TransUnion and Thomson Reuters. Therefore, due to the gap between these large players and RDVT, it is more reasonable to examine the company's key metrics in comparison with the sector. As the table below indicates, RDVT appears to outperform the sector in all key ratios. However, I want to highlight two of them: Net income margin and Return on Capital.

Starting with Return on Capital, it indicates that RDVT uses its capital very efficiently; considering its low debt level, this reflects the company's ability to generate high profits using primarily its own capital. This has a positive impact on its profitability, as reflected in the Net income Margin, which is just impressive. Both metrics indicate a company with a highly efficient and profitable business model.

Hidden Assets

A hidden asset of RDVT is the growth in revenue related to unsatisfied or partially unsatisfied obligations from pricing contracts (backlog) – for example, the backlog for FY 2024 is presented on page 26 of the annual report. As you can see in the table below, the backlog has followed a similar upward trend alongside the company's revenue growth. This is a sign that the company will maintain a stable revenue stream for the upcoming years.

Clients can access the massive dataset that RDVT holds and leverage the AI/ML tools the company has developed, placing them in a strong position to make real-time decisions and perform advanced analytics. Furthermore, RDVT offers its customers the opportunity for custom integrations, allowing them to connect their internal systems (i.e. ERP) with the company's data platform. All of the above make RDVT's business model more flexible, create opportunities to attract new customers and indicate its advanced technological infrastructure – one more hidden asset that investors should take into consideration.

Valuation Metrics and Target Price

Key Metrics

In order to determine the best assumption for the valuation calculation, I based it on the company's revenue and growth momentum, rather than a comparison with its peers. The reason behind this is that TRI and TRU operate on a different business scale. Therefore, investors need to understand whether RDVT's already exceptional revenue growth still has more room to continue. I found the answer in its Q1 2025 report. Specifically, RDVT reported an impressive increase of 26% in total revenue as of March 31, 2025, compared to the three months ended March 31, 2024. An even stronger signal about the company's positive momentum is the 93% increase in net income, as illustrated in the table below.

Target Price

RDVT's target price is calculated using DCF and EV/EBITDA models.

DCF Model

Taking into account the CAGR of 25.5% for the data analytics industry and the CAGR of 21.2% for the fraud detection market, as mentioned above, an average CAGR estimate of 16% through 2030 seems reasonable and conservative. Also, the projected CAGR is in line with the company's historical growth, as calculated from FY 2019 to FY 2024.

In order to achieve the most reliable growth rate assumptions for the key components of the DCF calculation, historical data from the six most recent years was used. The table below illustrates the average percentage of revenue for each component, as calculated over the same period.

Finally, adjustments for non-cash charges, such as depreciation—stock-based compensation expense and deferred income taxes—were also taken into consideration, using the same approach as described above to calculate the final DCF target price. The following table summarizes the key assumptions and methodology used to arrive at the DCF valuation results:

EV/EBITDA Model

According to Seeking Alpha's valuation metrics, RDVT's trailing EV/EBITDA ratio is 59.74, which is above the sector's median by 257.48% (sector median is 16.71) and below the company's 5-year average by 92.91% (RDVT average is 843.13). However, since we have annual data only from FY 2018, the 5-year average is not representative, considering that RDVT is still a relatively new player in the industry. Therefore, I based my analysis on the average EV/EBITDA multiple over the most recent quarters, as illustrated in the table below. A ratio of 25% appears more reasonable for valuation calculations.

The above revenue growth and valuation ratios are based on the assumptions that tariffs will remain at a low-level range and no crucial geopolitical event will occur over the next five years. Also, a WACC of 5% and an effective tax rate of 25% were used to calculate the implied enterprise value. The following table summarizes the key assumptions and methodology used to arrive at the EV/EBITDA valuation results:

Based on the above models, RDVT's target share price is summarized as follows:

However, given the strong growth potential of the data analytics and fraud detection industries, it is more reasonable to have more confidence in the DCF results. For this reason, the weighted average of the above price estimates has been selected and the final target share price is $84.51.

Risk Analysis

Risk analysis includes two main risks: High dependence on Core Platform and Competition.

High dependence on Core Platform

As an investor, I need to mention that RDVT has a high dependence on its Core Platform regarding its revenue streams. Almost all of its revenue comes from the Core Platform or from sub-software b that relies on this platform. Therefore, any issue or operating interruption of this platform could lead to a loss of customers' trust. Even more, if a competitor develops a product or AI service with capabilities similar to the Core Platform, it could lead RDVT to face a decline in revenue.

Competition

As already mentioned, RDVT operates in a very competitive industry with already large and well-established players. Therefore, its products and services must be able to differentiate the company from its competitors to attract new customers. It is very possible that RDVT will face price pressure, which means lower revenue growth and profitability. RDVT needs to increase its marketing expenses in order to build a stronger brand name and attract new clients. Also, large players offer more holistic solutions to their clients – including financial, tax and digital transformation services. This is a factor that RDVT cannot easily compete with, and it may reduce its ability to attract new customers.

Conclusion

Strong financials, consistent revenue growth, and high profitability indicate room for further expansion. RDVT's Q1 2025 results are proof that its business model has both momentum and capability to sustain its growth trend. The company operates in a highly competitive industry with larger and well-established players; however, it has already gained market share due to its technologically advanced solutions. Overall, RDVT appears to be an undervalued company with a proven, high-performing business model that operates in an industry expected to see solid growth. Therefore, in my opinion, Red Violet is a Buy.

data.title: Oklo: Continued Failure To Live Up To The Hype
data.description:
Monday's mixed shelf offering of up to $1B is the latest indication that Oklo Inc. (NYSE:OKLO) is not executing according to the plan they shared when they SPAC’d. They have been burning cash, and at the current rate had, before the offering, 1-3 years worth of funding left. What’s more, the timeline for the deployment of their first plant has been pushed back (now to late 2027/28). While further dilution might be off the table for now, there are reasons to doubt the company’s ambitious roadmap and strategy.

The stock price has recently ripped (again) on the back of Trump’s push for faster SMR project approvals. It's unclear what the impact will be, as Oklo has yet to even resubmit a Combined Construction and Operating License Application (COLA). Their initial COLA was denied in 2022 due to "significant information gaps" in the description of the reactor's potential accidents and safety system classifications.

It’s also worthwhile to note that Biden made efforts through the Advance Act to speed up project approval and provided the NRC with more funding to review projects; under Trump, this capacity may have been reduced. The US President's initial discretionary budget request for fiscal 2026 cuts $4.7B from the Department of Energy's federal funding, including a $408M from the Office of Nuclear Energy. Though funding remains for what the Trump admin considers essential to national nuclear leadership, including reactor development, advanced nuclear fuels, and support for the Idaho National Laboratory.

Insiders don't appear to be optimistic. Michael Klein, who sponsored the SPAC, sold most of his shares (96%) in February 2025, booking a huge profit. He has a series of SPACs to his name, and his de-SPAC companies have pretty terrible returns. Just look at the charts of Clarivate, SkillSoft, MultiPlan, and Lucid Motors. Sam Altman sold a third of his shares in April (at prices far lower than today's) and stepped down as chairman.

Oklo recently struck a deal with Korea Hydro & Nuclear Power to jointly advance the development of Oklo’s Aurora powerhouse and cooperate on early-stage project development efforts, including manufacturability assessments, supply chain development, and constructability planning. This deal calls into question whether Oklo still plans to stick to their original business plan that involves owning the technology, building the projects, and managing the plants. They seem to be instead veering toward the typical model for power producers of partnering with another firm for the construction and management of plants. Oklo management may have decided their publicized build-own-operate plan makes execution extremely challenging – better to focus on what they do best, which is to design the technology.

Oklo's Ambitious & Questionable Plan

As if building and operating nuclear plants isn’t hard enough, Oklo has declared their ambition to build a rare type of plant (small-scale), using novel technology (sodium-cooled fast reactor), employing an untested business plan that involves building and operating their own plants.

They started with plans to build a tiny 1.5 MW reactor to power remote communities and outposts. After their first application was denied by the NRC, they moved on to a 15 MW design, then 50MW, 75MW, and 100MW designs. Why do they keep increasing the size of their proposed reactors? Likely because small reactors are cost-prohibitive; cost-effectiveness is achieved with scale or mass production (which Oklo is very far from achieving today).

What about their project backlog? Oklo has a project pipeline of 2.1 GW to power data centers. They have signed letters of intent for these projects. Importantly, these are non-binding agreements. They also have a separate agreement with data center developer Switch to deploy 12 GW of projects through 2044. Again, non-binding, and no concrete agreements related to this soft agreement have been signed.

Oklo has a lot of support from tech leaders and investors. Clearly, there is a lot of optimism surrounding this company. But the project-level economics is where this story really comes apart.

Dubious Project Economics

In their S-4 filings, Oklo estimates construction costs for their 15 MW first-of-a-kind reactor to be $34M and HALEU fuel costs of $35M, purchased from a third-party supplier for $7,000/kg. As they gain experience, they expect to reduce construction costs to $24M and fuel costs to $33M. For the 50 MW reactor, they expect to spend $86M to build them and $56M to fuel them, decreasing to $61M and $55M with experience. As a result, the company expects to deliver a levelized cost of electricity in the range of $40/MWh to $90/MWh, which is quite low.

According to a Lazard analysis, Oklo's Aurora reactor will cost around $70M for the 15 MW version, with a levelized cost of electricity of $80-$130/MWh, similar to peaking gas-fired plants and offshore wind.

In contrast, the US Department of Energy provides cost estimates for first-of-a-kind nuclear projects in the US, showing an overnight cost of capital (total cost required to build) of $10,000/KW. This would translate to a $150M build cost for Oklo’s smallest 15 MW reactor.

This compares unfavorably to the GE Hitachi (BWRX-300) estimated cost of $60/MWh, which benefits from a simplified design and existing supply chain. The BWRX-300 is a 300 MW reactor design that is actually being built today.

While there are some significant discrepancies between Oklo’s estimates and construction cost estimates from third parties, the disparity is even more stark when it comes to HALEU fuel costs. It’s unclear how Oklo expects to pay $7,000/kg. The Nuclear Innovation Alliance estimates HALEU costs about $26,000/kg.

Calculated HALEU production cost for uranium enriched to 19.75% is $23,725 / kgU for HALEU in an oxide form and $25,725 for HALEU in a metallic form under baseline economic assumptions but could be higher.

The cost of HALEU has a huge impact on project feasibility. Oklo touts the 10-year life of fuel as a major benefit of their design over conventional nuclear reactors that need to be refueled more frequently, as it lowers operating costs. But if the cost of fuel is very high, and you need a lot of it, this perceived advantage turns into a problem. Even at the low cost quoted by Oklo, the fuel cost makes up half the total project costs for the 15 MW reactor.

Even more problematic is the fact that a HALEU fuel supply chain does not currently exist. Today, if you want HALEU you have to buy it from TENEX, a Russian-state-owned entity. Oklo does have a grant from the Idaho National Laboratory to cover initial fueling costs for their first 5 metric tons, but this will only cover one unit. Centrus just began producing HALEU in Piketon, Ohio, producing their first 20 kg in November 2023, compared to the 5,000 kg needed for one Oklo reactor. But they still import most of their underlying uranium used to make HALEU from Russia and elsewhere.

If HALEU fuel costs are actually 4-5x higher than the company claims, Oklo’s actual levelized cost of electricity would be more than double the levelized cost of electricity they claim they can achieve.
